coredata.dc:title: Construction of NCQDs/Ag3PO4/Ag composite with dual-channel charge transfer property for efficient photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin
coredata.dc:creator: Yao, Yu
Lu, Chengqi
Shen, Qianhong
Yang, Yijin
Huang, Kaiyue
Jiang, Libei
Liu, Jie
Li, Yue
Cao, Xudan
Yang, Hui
coredata.dc:description: A novel ternary composite photocatalyst of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)/tetrapods silver phosphate (Ag3PO4)/silver (Ag) nanoparticles was designed and fabricated. Kelvin probe force microscopy and in-situ irradiated X-ray photoelectron spectrum demonstrated the cooperative action between the S-scheme heterojunction and Schottky junction within this ternary composite system, which facilitate efficient dual-channel charge transfer and achieve high oxidation potential. Moreover, the incorporating of persulfate into the photocatalytic reaction system enhanced the capture of photogenerated electrons, while the generated SO4 -· radicals further improved photocatalytic reaction efficiency. Consequently, the NCQDs/Ag3PO4/Ag photocatalyst exhibited remarkable photocatalytic performance for the removal of ciprofloxacin (CIP). The mineralization rate of CIP over NCQDs/Ag3PO4/Ag photocatalyst activated by persulfate was about 6.7 times greater compared to the bare Ag3PO4 photocatalyst. Additionally, four potential pathways for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP were proposed utilizing the LC-MS analysis. Furthermore, this ternary composite photocatalyst not only demonstrated high cyclic stability but also exhibited robust photocatalytic activity in systems containing coexisting anions, cations or various natural water sources, thereby exhibiting its application potential for antibiotic degradation in real environmental settings.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ternary composite photocatalyst of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)/tetrapods silver phosphate (Ag3PO4)/silver (Ag) nanoparticles was designed and fabricated. Kelvin probe force microscopy and in-situ irradiated X-ray photoelectron spectrum demonstrated the cooperative action between the S-scheme heterojunction and Schottky junction within this ternary composite system, which facilitate efficient dual-channel charge transfer and achieve high oxidation potential. Moreover, the incorporating of persulfate into the photocatalytic reaction system enhanced the capture of photogenerated electrons, while the generated SO4 -· radicals further improved photocatalytic reaction efficiency. Consequently, the NCQDs/Ag3PO4/Ag photocatalyst exhibited remarkable photocatalytic performance for the removal of ciprofloxacin (CIP). The mineralization rate of CIP over NCQDs/Ag3PO4/Ag photocatalyst activated by persulfate was about 6.7 times greater compared to the bare Ag3PO4 photocatalyst. Additionally, four potential pathways for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP were proposed utilizing the LC-MS analysis. Furthermore, this ternary composite photocatalyst not only demonstrated high cyclic stability but also exhibited robust photocatalytic activity in systems containing coexisting anions, cations or various natural water sources, thereby exhibiting its application potential for antibiotic degradation in real environmental settings.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the widespread application of antibiotics in disease treatment, these substances have become a typical pollutant in the ecological environment. They persistently accumulate in natural water bodies and are challenging to eliminate, posing serious threats to both human health and the ecological environment [1]. Ciprofloxacin (CIP), one of the third-generation broad-spectrum antibiotics, is extensively utilized across human medicine, agriculture, forestry, and animal husbandry because of its high antibacterial properties. However, due to its special structure (piperazine ring and quinolone ring, etc.), it is challenging to achieve complete degradation of CIP without secondary contamination by traditional methods [2]. Therefore, CIP residues in natural waterbodies have become a major threat to the ecosystems. There is an urgent need for efficient, green, stable, and sustainable technologies capable of achieving effective CIP degradation. Photocatalytic oxidation technology offers a promising approach for degrading organic pollutants under mild and environmentally friendly conditions without generating secondary pollution. Many photocatalysts have demonstrated good activity for CIP [3], but how to further improve the quantum efficiency and mineralization ability of these photocatalysts is still a challenging problem [4].

Ag3PO4 photocatalyst has attracted extensive attention for its efficient photocatalytic degradation of CIP, owing to its outstanding visible-light response and deep valence band potential [5–7]. However, the electron-phonon interaction in narrow bandgap semiconductors leads to a reduced lifetime of photogenerated carriers [8]. Moreover, it is generally challenging to achieve both effective light absorption and appropriate band position alignment simultaneously in narrow bandgap semiconductor photocatalysts. As the conduction band (CB) position of Ag3PO4 (0.52 eV vs NHE) has a value greater than the standard potential of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV vs NHE), photo-induced electrons (e−) are unable to react with O2 to generate ·O2 − radicals, which are the primary reactive species involved in photocatalytic reactions. Consequently, heightening the separation and utilization of photo-induced electron-hole pairs has emerged as a critical focus for advancing Ag3PO4-based photocatalysts [9].

Semiconductor heterojunction can significantly enhance the transfer efficiency of photo-induced carriers. There are mainly three different charge transfer mechanisms: traditional II-scheme, Z-scheme, and S-scheme. Among them, while the traditional II-scheme heterojunctions effectively promote the transfer of photo-induced electron-hole pairs, their constructed band structures are often considered unfavorable for activating various reactive radicals. To address the limitations associated with traditional II-scheme heterojunctions, research has increasingly focused on developing Z-scheme heterojunctions. These improve the transfer efficiency of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, as well as maintaining excellent redox capabilities [10]. Furthermore, Yu et al. [11] introduced a novel step-scheme (S-scheme) heterojunction where one semiconductor has a higher Fermi level as a reductant and the other has a lower Fermi level as an oxidant, thus realizing the high-efficiency transfer of photo-induced carriers with strong redox property. Li et al. [12] constructed In2Se3/Ag3PO4 S-scheme heterojunction for application in hydrogen evolution and antibiotic wastewater degradation. The transfer efficiency and lifetime of photo-induced electron-hole pairs in S-scheme photocatalyst nanocomposites were found to be significantly superior to those observed in single Ag3PO4 or In2Se3. Recently, the integration of different types of heterojunctions has attracted more and more attention due to its potential to markedly enhance charge transfer efficiency and extend carrier lifetime [13]. Xia et al. [14] proposed an SS strategy involving 0D/2D Schottky junctions and 2D/2D S-scheme heterojunctions, and the constructed CNQDs/TCN/ZnIn2S4 photocatalyst exhibited strong photogenerated charge separation ability. In our previous works [15], we developed the Ag3PO4/Ag/AgX photocatalyst, which exhibits a tandem mechanism between S-scheme heterojunction and Schottky junction. This design significantly promoted the rapid transfer of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, resulting in outstanding performance for norfloxacin.

Herein, a ternary composite photocatalyst consisting of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs), tetrapods silver phosphate (Ag3PO4), and silver (Ag) nanoparticles was synthesized to construct NCQDs/Ag3PO4 S-scheme heterojunction and Ag3PO4/Ag Schottky junction, creating dual channels for separating photogenerated charges. Additionally, persulfate (PS), was introduced into the photocatalytic reaction system to enhance photocatalytic efficiency by capturing photogenerated electrons to generate SO4 −· and hydroxyl radical (·OH). The structure-property relationship of the composite photocatalyst was investigated, confirming its dual-channel charge transfer mechanism. Moreover, the influence factors and pathways of CIP photocatalytic degradation over the ternary composite photocatalyst were also studied to provide experimental support for its degradation in CIP antibiotics.

Ciprofloxacin hydrochloride (CIP, >97 %) and nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) were obtained from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphoric acid (H3PO4, >85 %), ethylenediamine (C2H8N2), potassium persulfate (PS, AR), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), terephthalic acid (TA, AR), ethanol (C2H6O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), silver nitrate (AgNO3, >99 %), potassium chloride (KCl), ammonium chloride (NH4Cl), methylene blue (MB), and methyl alcohol (CH3OH) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), urea (CH4N2O, AR), citric acid (C6H8O7, AR), sodium nitrate (NaNO3), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO, 97 %), sodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), benzoquinone (BZQ), sodium chloride (NaCl), barium chloride (BaCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), isopropanol (IPA), and sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4) were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All experiments were carried out with deionized water.

Synthesis of tetrapods Ag3PO4 used hydrothermal approach. A certain amount of AgNO3, H3PO4, and urea were dissolved into 80 mL of H2O and mixed thoroughly before being uniformly heated at 100 °C in a liner autoclave for an hour. The resulting yellow precipitate was obtained, washed, and then subjected to a 24-h drying process at 70 °C. The obtained sample was labeled as APO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: Ethylenediamine and citric acid were added in 10 mL of H2O and stirred to produce a clear solution. Then the solution was placed in a polytetrafluoroethylene container and kept at 200 °C for four hours. Finally, NCQDs were obtained from the brown solution through centrifugation.

The binary sample NCQDs/Ag3PO4 was synthesized using the low-temperature impregnation method. 100 mg of APO was homogenized by ultrasonication for half an hour. Subsequently, 0.25, 0.75, and 1.25 mL of NCQDs was introduced via dropwise addition with continuous stirring over a 24-h period, respectively. After transferring the resulting sample to a Muffle furnace, it was maintained at 200 °C for 24 h. Through the photocatalytic degradation performance test (Fig. S1 and Table S1), it was found that the loading volume of 0.75 % NCQDs was the best, and the sample was labeled as NCAPO and used for the subsequent preparation of ternary photocatalyst.

The ternary composite was prepared using a photoreduction method to load Ag nanoparticles onto the binary photocatalyst NCAPO. Specifically, AgNO3 was added into a solution containing uniform NCAPO, followed by irradiation under light for 2 h. Depending on the amount of AgNO3 used, the prepared samples were labeled as NCAPO-A5 (weight radio Ag: NCAPO = 5 %), NCAPO-A10 (weight radio Ag: NCAPO = 10 %), and NCAPO-A15 (weight radio Ag: NCAPO = 15 %). For comparative analysis, the binary photocatalyst Ag3PO4/Ag was prepared by a similar method with APO replacement, and the obtained sample was labeled as AAP.
A detailed account of this experimental characterization and analysis methods is provided in section Text S1 of the supplemental material.

The experimental procedures including photocatalysis experiment, experiments on different factors affecting CIP degradation, and reactive species capturing experiment are detailed in section Text S2 of the supplemental material.

The X-ray diffraction spectroscopy was conducted on the Ag3PO4 sample and samples modified by NCQDs and Ag nanoparticles. The results in Fig. 1 show that all as-prepared samples exhibit characteristic peaks corresponding to the body-centered cubic structure (JCPDS Card No. 06–0505). However, due to the small amount of NCQDs, its characteristic diffraction peak is not observed. Upon closer examination of the diffraction peaks between 37.2 and 38.7 degrees, it is evident that the (111) characteristic peak of Ag nanoparticles is present in all the ternary composite samples, with its intensity increasing with higher Ag content. Additionally, Raman spectra in Fig. 1c-d further illustrate the vibrational demonstrated groups present in the various samples. The sharp peak located at 904 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrational stretching of O-P-O in [PO4] group [16], which is observed in all samples containing Ag3PO4. Moreover, characteristic peaks of NCQDs at 1322 and 1393 cm−1 are associated with the D band and G band, respectively, suggesting vibrations from disordered sp3 and ordered sp2 hybrid carbon atoms within NCQDs [17]. Both XRD and Raman spectra successfully confirm combination of NCQDs on the surface of Ag3PO4 as well as the successful modification of Ag nanoparticles to establish a ternary composite sample.

The morphology of the as-prepared photocatalysts was conducted utilizing SEM and TEM images. Based on Fig. S2 and Fig. 2 , it is evident that Ag3PO4 maintains its tetrapods morphology after being combined with NCQDs and Ag nanoparticles, with an arm length of approximately 5 μm and width of 2 μm. Additionally, the composite samples exhibit increased surface roughness compared to pure Ag3PO4, providing more reactive sites. EDS mappings in both SEM and TEM images confirm the elemental distribution in sample NCAPO-A10, with the five major elements being Ag, P, O, C, and N, demonstrating the introduction of NCQDs and Ag. Fig. 2 k illustrates the 0.35 nm lattice spacing as the (101) plane of Ag nanoparticles [18] and the 0.21 nm lattice spacing as the (100) crystal plane of NCQDs [19], confirming the formation of NCQDs/Ag3PO4 heterojunction and Ag3PO4/Ag Schottky junction.

The constitution and chemical environment of each element were examined through high-resolution XPS spectral analysis. The alteration in electron density across various samples is demonstrated by the shift in the binding energy of the elements [20,21]. In Fig. 3b, the presence of distinct peaks near 368.2 and 374.3 eV is attributed to Ag+ in the APO [22]. However, these two peaks exhibit a slight negative shift in the NCAPO due to the construction of a heterojunction between Ag3PO4 and NCQDs interface. Other flat peaks positioned at 368.6 and 374.6 eV in the sample AAP are associated with elemental silver (Ag0) [23], indicating successful decoration of Ag nanoparticles onto APO. In the ternary NCAPO-A10, four peaks related to the Ag 3d are observed, displaying similar phenomena and regularity. The strong peak at 133.0 eV in the P 2p spectra is ascribed to P5+ in the Ag3PO4 [24], which undergoes a negative shift in NCAPO and a positive shift in AAP. This suggests the creation of an S-scheme junction on the surface of Ag3PO4 and NCQDs, as well as the emergence of a Schottky junction between Ag3PO4 and Ag. According to Fig. 3d, the O 1s signal at 530.9 and 532.0 eV in spectra can be correlated to PO and OH, respectively [25]. Another peak located at 530.2 eV appears in sample NCAPO, which is associated with the CO radical [26]. This proves that NCQDs have been effectively attached to Ag3PO4. Similarly, the three peaks fitted in the O 1s spectra of the ternary NCAPO-A10 exhibit the oxygen function groups. Moreover, analyzing the C 1s signal data (Fig. 3e) for the NCQDs, the four peaks at 284.4, 285.8, 286.5, and 288.6 eV are attributed to C-C/C=C, CN, CO, and CCO bonds, respectively [27]. In composite samples, these four peaks exhibit similar positive shifts, reflecting a reduction in the electron concentration of NCQDs. As shown in Fig. 3f, the N 1s XPS spectra of NCQDs reveal major peaks at 399.1, 399.7, and 400.8 eV, which are associated with C=N-O, CN, and CN bonds, respectively [28,29]. Furthermore, the slight positive shifts of these three N 1s spectral peaks in the composite samples further confirm the construction of the S-scheme junction.

The photocatalytic performance of the fabricated samples was measured in the presence of PS, with CIP selected as the target degradation substance. The samples were exposed to irradiation for 80 min under a Xenon lamp with a cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm). As depicted in Fig. 4a, pure PS shows no photocatalytic property, whereas the addition of PS over APO results in a CIP degradation rate of 56.02 %. After Ag nanoparticles are loaded with tetrapods Ag3PO4, the photocatalytic degradation of CIP increases to 65.68 %. Furthermore, the degradation rate of CIP increases to 67.18 % after NCQDs modification of Ag3PO4. In ternary composite samples, NCAPO-A10 doped with 10 % Ag nanoparticles has the highest photocatalytic degradation rate for CIP at 88.72 %. This proves that synergetic junctions between NCQDs and Ag significantly boost the redox ability of the ternary sample and inhibit the photo-corrosion of Ag3PO4. To further elucidate the influence of photodynamic in the photocatalytic reaction process, pseudo-zero-order, pseudo-first-order, and pseudo-second-order kinetics are fitted as follows [30]:

where k₀ (mg/L min−1), k1 (min−1), and k2 (L mg−1 min−1) are the zero, first, and second-order kinetic rate constant, respectively. C0 and Ct (mg/L) are the initial and final concentrations of CIP at time t. Based on Fig. 4b, Fig. S3, and Table S2, the second-order kinetic constant is the most suitable choice due to its highest correlation coefficient (R2) among the three options. The second-order k2 value of the NCAPO-A10 (0.0967 L mg−1 min−1) is about 3.31 times that of the APO (0.0292 L mg−1 min−1), confirming that NCQDs generate more reactive radicals when combined with Ag3PO4 and Ag nanoparticles absorb a large number of electrons to produce more reactive species for accelerating degradation. Besides, Table. S3 demonstrates superior performance compared to other commonly used Ag3PO4-based photocatalysts in previous studies for degrading CIP. The NCAPO-A10 exhibits excellent photocatalytic activity, attributed to the advantageous dual-channel functionality of S-scheme heterojunction and Schottky junction, leading to rapid decomposition of CIP in the photocatalytic system after PS activation.

Furthermore, the mineralization rate of CIP photocatalyzed by the ternary composite NCAPO-A10 (19.8 %) demonstrates a 2.41-fold increase compared to that of the pure sample APO (8.2 %) without adding persulfate, as depicted in Fig. 4c. Under identical conditions, the photocatalytic mineralization rate of ciprofloxacin significantly rises to 54.9 % with the addition of PS into the composite system. These results indicate that persulfate can effectively decompose CIP intermediates during the photocatalysis process. When the photocatalytic duration is further extended to 4 h, according to Fig. 4d, the degradation rate of ciprofloxacin reaches nearly 100 %, with the mineralization rate increasing to 73.7 %. This suggests that the composite photocatalytic system shows potential for practical application in water treatment.

The stability and recyclability of the NCAPO-A10 were evaluated through five consecutive photocatalytic degradation experiments. The data in Fig. 4e reveals that the degradation rate of ciprofloxacin remains at 86.73 % after five cycles, indicating satisfactory stability of the photocatalyst. In addition, the XRD pattern displays high crystallinity of the sample after cyclic experimentation, similar to the fresh sample, suggesting that the modified ternary photocatalyst with NCQDs and Ag shows potential for the degradation of antibiotics in wastewater.

The light absorption capability of the photocatalysts was recorded in the UV–vis absorption spectral data. Referring to Fig. 5a, the composite sample exhibits a noticeable redshift in the optical band edge compared to the single sample, expanding its light absorption range and enhancing photocatalytic degradation ability [31]. Further analysis reveals that composite samples containing NCQDs show a minor peak at about 330 nm, associated with the π-π* and n-π* transitions in sp2 regions [32,33]. Following the Kubelka-Munk Eq. [34], the band gaps of the APO and NCQDs were calculated as 2.12 and 2.84 eV, respectively. The combination of narrow (APO) and wide (NCQDs) band gap semiconductors is expected to regulate the VB and CB position of the composite photocatalyst, facilitating efficient utilization of reactive radicals (h+, ·OH, ·O2 −, and SO4 −·, etc.) while inhibiting recombination of photogenerated e−/h+, ultimately improving activity.

The electrochemical activities of the samples were characterized using EIS and i-t spectroscopy, which can confirm the differences in the ability to separate photogenerated e−/h+ in distinct photocatalytic systems. Based on the EIS spectrum, the smaller arc radius indicates a stronger separation ability of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, while a larger arc radius suggests a higher photogenerated carrier recombination ratio [35]. Based on this property, the recombination of photogenerated carriers in the composite samples is significantly inhibited compared to APO (Fig. 5d), thereby enhancing the photocatalytic performance. Moreover, the NCAPO-A10 exhibits the highest and most stable photocurrent intensity over six consecutive on-off cycles under visible light irradiation (Fig. 5e). This observation signifies that the anchoring of NCQDs and the loading of silver nanoparticles promote the efficient separation of photogenerated carriers, ultimately optimizing the photocatalytic properties [36].

The phenomenon of fluorescence quenching observed during the photocatalysis process is originated from the recombination of photo-induced carriers within the semiconductors. Therefore, a reduction in fluorescence intensity within the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum indicates a decrease in the carrier recombination rate [37]. Fig. 5f displays the PL curves for various samples, revealing a marked decline in PL intensity for NCAPO compared to APO. This result suggests that the heterojunction constructed between NCQDs and Ag3PO4 effectively improves the carrier separation efficiency. Furthermore, the presence of Ag nanoparticles further reduces fluorescence intensity in the ternary photocatalytic system, indicating that the Schottky junction facilitates efficient electron transfer and inhibits reintegration of photo-induced e−/h+. To further investigate the dynamics of photogenerated carrier separation, the lifetimes of the synthesized photocatalysts were assessed via time-resolved fluorescent spectroscopy based on τ =

[38]. Following Table S4 and Fig. S4, the average lifetime of NCAPO-A10 (1.05 ns) is 3.55 times longer than that of APO (0.29 ns), indicating an increased concentration of electron-hole pairs associated with photocatalysis, and resulting in an extended fluorescence lifetime for the composite sample.

The electronic band structure, specifically the positioning of the valence and conduction bands, plays a crucial role in determining the photocatalytic activity. The type of the semiconductor and the Fermi level (EF) are measured by the Mott-Schottky (M-S) curve. As can be seen from Fig. S5a-b, both APO and NCQDs are n-type semiconductors due to the upward trend of their M-S curve. In addition, the Fermi levels of APO and NCQDs are approximately 0.32 and − 0.03 V (vs SCE), respectively, calculated from the X-intercept of the tangent line on the M-S curve. By employing formula [39]: E

+ 0.24 , it is possible to calculate that the Fermi levels of APO and NCQDs are 0.56 and 0.21 V (vs NHE) individually, representing the primary drivers of electron transfer. The binary NCAPO, AAP, and the ternary NCAPO-A10 all form excellent Mott-Schottky structure, which facilitate efficient electron transfer. VB-XPS spectra were used to measure the energy gap between EF and VB of the semiconductor [40]. The valence band positions for APO and NCQDs, as demonstrated in Fig. S6, are 2.08 and 0.72 eV, respectively. Therefore, the valence band energy (EVB) of APO and NCQDs is determined to be 2.64 and 0.93 eV (vs NHE), respectively. The formula

enables the calculation of the conduction band energy (ECB) [41]. Consequently, the ECB of the as-prepared APO and NCQDs are computed as 0.52 and − 1.91 eV (vs NHE), respectively. The specific details regarding the VB and CB of APO and NCQDs are presented in Fig. S5f.

Experiments for trapping primary reactive species, involved in the NCAPO-A10 photocatalytic system, were executed to using trapping agents BZQ, IPA, EDTA-2Na, and methanol for capturing h+, ·O2 −, ·OH, and SO4 -·generated during photocatalysis, respectively. The addition of these scavenging agents results in a noticeable decrease in the photocatalytic removal rate of CIP, with the result in Fig. 6a. Moreover, h+ is identified as the most crucial reactive radical, as NCAPO-A10 loses its degradation ability after EDTA-2Na captures h+. Both ·O2 − and ·OH species participate in the photocatalytic reaction and play specific roles. It is well established that methanol can capture both SO4 -·and ·OH. To clarify the differences in their contributions, the second-order k2 value was used to calculate the degree of contribution for each group [42], as displayed in Eq. (4)–(5).

where kNCAPO-A10, kIPA, and kmethanol are the second-order kinetic constant in the CIP degradation process without scavengers, with IPA and methanol, respectively.

Hence, referring to Fig. 6b, the second-order k2 value indicates that the contribution degree of ·OH and SO4 −·for CIP degradation is calculated as 68.87 % and 17.61 %, respectively. To further elucidate their influence, ESR spectra with DMPO as a probe were employed with visible light exposure. Fig. 6c represents the prominent signals of ·OH and SO4 -·, with the former exhibiting a higher intensity than the latter. This confirms that ·OH has a more crucial contribution than SO4 -·, aligning with the finding of the capture experiment.

The generation of ·O2 − during photocatalysis can be further confirmed by the change of the UV–visible maximum absorption peak (259 nm) of NBT [43]. The non-generation of ·O2 − in pure Ag3PO4, as demonstrated by Fig. 6d, can be explained by the CB position of Ag3PO4 (0.52 eV), which is positioned at a higher potential than the standard reduction potential of O2/·O2 − (−0.33 eV) [44]. According to Fig. 6e-f, after combination with NCQDs, the NBT transformation rate reaches 28.24 %, indicating the generation of superoxide radicals in the NCAPO photocatalytic reactions, which is in accordance with the scavenging result. It is suggested that the CB of NCQDs (−1.91 eV) exhibits a sufficiently negative potential to generate ·O2 −. Furthermore, the NCAPO-A10 is covered with silver nanoparticles, which boost the transfer ratio of NBT, indicating an increased generation of ·O2 − in the ternary photocatalytic system. The creation of a Schottky junction upon contact of Ag with Ag3PO4 is demonstrated to promote electron transfer and facilitate the production of a considerable amount of ·O2 − [45]. The possible mechanism is inferred as Eq. (6)–(7):
(6)
Ag + hv → Ag
e −
+ Ag

(7)

O 2
→ ·
O 2 −

Moreover, the existence of the hydroxyl group generated during the photocatalytic process can be detected by terephthalic acid (TA), which reacts with ·OH to produce 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid with enhanced fluorescence intensity [46]. Fig. 6g-i illustrates the relationship between the PL intensity of different photocatalysts and irradiation time, supporting that APO, NCAPO, and NCAPO-A10 all generate ·OH after 80 min of illumination, with the composite system exhibiting a higher level of ·OH production. It further validates that ternary NCAPO-A10 can produce a larger amount ·OH stably. This suggests that integrating NCQDs with Ag nanoparticles facilitates better separation of photo-induced carriers and increases the generation of ·OH in the composite heterojunction [47].

Based on the analysis and characterization of the above materials, it can be inferred that the higher Fermi level of the NCQDs compared to Ag3PO4 results in electron transfer from NCQDs to Ag3PO4 after contact until their Fermi levels equalize [48]. Accordingly, the surface potential of the NCQDs increases while that of the Ag3PO4 decreases. The Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) diagrams of Fig. S7e demonstrate that before and after contact, the surface potential of the NCQDs increases from 8.5 mV to 29 mV, while the surface potential of the Ag3PO4 decreases from 89 mV to 29 mV. After contact, KPFM results confirm that Ag3PO4 gains electrons and its surface potential decreases, while NCQDs lose electrons and their potential increases. This indicates that an S-scheme heterojunction is formed between Ag3PO4 and NCQDs. Similarly, in Fig. S7f, it is demonstrated that the surface potential of the binary photocatalyst AAP increases from 89 mV to 244 mV compared to single Ag3PO4, indicating the capture of electrons on the surface of Ag3PO4 by Ag nanoparticles and formation of a Schottky junction.

To further elucidate the electron transfer pathways under light irradiation, in-situ irradiated XPS characterization (ISI-XPS) was conducted in Fig. S8. A rise in Ag and P binding energy of Ag3PO4 is attributed to the loss of electrons, in contrast to conditions in darkness. Conversely, the lowering of the binding energies for C and N is a result of gaining electrons. Therefore, under the light irradiation, the photo-induced e− at CB of the Ag3PO4 combines with photo-induced h+ at VB of the NCQDs in the internal electric field (IEF), which matches the S-scheme charge transfer pathway in the heterojunction [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation on the photocatalytic mechanism of NCAPO-A10 activated by PS
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the aforementioned analysis and evidence, a potential dual-channel synergetic mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of CIP over the NCAPO-A10 activated by PS is proposed as follows (Eq. (8)–(14)) and illustrated in Fig. 7 . When Ag3PO4 and NCQDs come into contact, electrons transfer from NCQDs to Ag3PO4, driven by IEF until the Fermi levels of the two converge. Additionally, Ag nanoparticles capture a large number of electrons under the action of Schottky junction, allowing ·O2 − to fully contribute to the photocatalytic reaction activities. The photogenerated electrons on the CB of Ag3PO4 could easily transfer to Ag nanoparticles (Ag3PO4 CB → Ag nanoparticles) through the Schottky junction owing to that the CB position of Ag3PO4 is more negative than the Fermi level of Ag (2.3 eV) [50]. Meanwhile, Electrons on the CB of Ag3PO4 combine with h+ on the VB of NCQDs under light to inhibit photo-corrosion of Ag3PO4 and effectively utilize reactive groups h+ and ·OH for CIP degradation [51]. Moreover, in the PS system, S2O8 2− can react with electrons to produce SO4 -·and directly participate in photocatalytic reactions or indirectly participate in reactions with H2O to form ·OH [52], achieving efficient removal of CIP through dual-channel effects of S-scheme heterojunction and Schottky junction in NCAPO-A10 photocatalyst activated by PS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the influencing factors in photocatalytic degradation of CIP

ce:section.ce:para: The concentration of persulfate has a direct impact on the production of reactive species and thereby the photocatalytic performance. According to Fig. 8a-c, Table S5, and Fig. S9, a higher concentration of persulfate is associated with a rising removal efficiency of CIP, as well as the second-order k2 value. However, there is no obvious subsequent improvement in effect, particularly when the persulfate concentration reaches 20 mM. This phenomenon may be attributed to the free radical self-burst [53], which inhibits the oxidizing capacity of PS. From an economic perspective, a concentration of 10 mM for PS would be preferable to choose.

As is widely recognized, the dosage of the photocatalyst is of considerable importance to the system, thus necessitating an exploration of this parameter. As depicted in Fig. 8d-f, Fig. S10, and Table S6, the removal rate of CIP increases with higher dosages and can achieve rapid degradation with a small dosage over a short period. However, continuous increases in dosage do not further improve the degradation rate of CIP, as they reach the upper limit capacity of the photocatalyst. Moreover, there is only a slight difference in the second-order k2 value for various dosages. Therefore, it can be concluded that 50 mg is the most optimal dosage for NCAPO-A10.

In practical application, the concentration of CIP in different water sources varies, so there is a need to research the effect of the CIP concentration during photocatalysis. According to Fig. 8g-i, Table S7, and Fig. S11, an increase in CIP concentration leads to a gradual decline in the photocatalytic activity. It is a result of the higher demand for reactive radicals at high CIP concentrations, with insufficient active substances limiting the efficient degradation of CIP.

The influence of acid-alkaline environmental conditions on the performance of photocatalysts has been investigated by examining the effect of initial solution pH on the degradation rate. It is observed that acid conditions accelerate the photocatalytic reaction, while alkaline conditions hinder the removal of CIP, as shown in Fig. 8j-l, Table S8, and Fig. S12. This is a result of the tendency in acidic conditions to produce reactive radical SO4 −·, which plays a crucial role during photocatalysis, whereas alkaline conditions tend to consume SO4 −·. The relevant explanation can be found in Eq. (15)–(17).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Coexisting anions, cations and different natural water matrixes
ce:section.ce:para:
The presence of common anions in natural water, such as NO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, HPO4 2−, and H2PO4 − can impact the generation and action of reactive species in photocatalysis. To research the influence of various anions in the photocatalytic system, these anions were introduced before the photocatalytic reaction. Fig. 9a-c, Table S9, and Fig. S13 show the varying degree of inhibition caused by different anions on the photocatalytic reaction, that is HPO4 2− > Cl− > SO4 2− > H2PO4 − > NO3 −. The second-order k2 value of these anions systems decreases compared to the system without the anions (0.0967 L mg−1 min−1), indicating inhibition of the photocatalytic reaction. HPO4 2− and H2PO4 − can consume reactive species and prevent contact between the photocatalyst and PS, thus impeding pollutant degradation. The addition of NO3 − generates another radical NO3 −· (2.30 eV, with lower redox potential), which is unfavorable for the photocatalytic oxidation reaction [54]. The addition of Cl− captures photogenerated holes and hydroxyl group, significantly hindering the function of reactive species [55]. Moreover, the addition of SO4 2− inhibits the formation of SO4 -·and reduces its reduction potential [56], which fails to promote the removal rate of CIP. The effects of various anions on the reactive species during photocatalysis are shown in Eq. (18)–(23).

Similarly, the effects of cations in the photocatalytic system were studied, as shown in Fig. 9d-f, Table. S10, and Fig. S14. Compared with the ternary photocatalytic system without ions, all the five cations inhibited the photocatalytic performance. Among them, Zn2+ inhibited the most. This may be because d orbitals of Zn2+ are not full and have the ability to capture electrons [57], which reduces the production of ·O2 − in the reaction and further inhibits the photocatalytic degradation performance.

To further investigate the influence of NCAPO-A10 on the photocatalytic decomposition of CIP in a natural water source, experiments were conducted using lake water, river water, wetland water, and tap water (Fig. S15 for detailed locations of water collection and Table S11 for ion concentration in the West Lake water). The results show that NCAPO-A10 maintains good photocatalytic performance for CIP degradation in various natural water sources. On the basis of Fig. 9g-i and Fig. S15f, the CIP removal rate remains at about 66 % in five different types of water matrices, except for tap water where it is only 58 %. Compared to the deionized water system, the decrease in CIP removal rate in natural water sources may be attributed to the presence of multiple anions (NO3 −, SO4 2−, and Cl−), which hinder the production and action of reactive radicals during the photocatalysis process. The lower CIP photocatalytic degradation rate observed in tap water may be due to higher levels of Cl− ions and an alkaline environment (pH = 8.4), which inhibit the photocatalytic elimination of CIP by h+ and ·OH groups. This finding is consistent with previous research on the effects of varying initial pH values and ions within the photocatalytic process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intermediates and possible photocatalytic degradation pathways of CIP
ce:section.ce:para: By employing liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS), potential intermediates (Table S13 and Fig. S17) of CIP photocatalytic degradation by the NCAPO-A10 were characterized, and potential degradation routes were inferred. CIP has a molecular mass (m/z) of approximately 332, with a complex structure consisting of a piperazine ring, quinolone ring, and cyclopropyl group [58]. Fig. 10 presents the LC-MS analysis outcomes, which include thirteen intermediate products and four potential degradation routes for CIP. In pathway I, the oxidative transformation of the piperazine ring and attack on the CF bond by the hydroxyl group occur [59], followed by the opening of the piperazine ring to generate P2 (m/z = 304.29). According to pathway II, ·OH attacks the carboxyl group attached to the quinolone group of the CIP molecule which is then replaced by the hydroxyl group [60]. Subsequently, oxidation and cleavage of the piperazine group form an amino group [61], while hydrogenation converts cyclopropyl to ethyl resulting in P4 (m/z = 206.15). This is followed by an attack on the quinolone group by ·O2 −, breaking the CC bond to produce P5 (m/z = 242.28) [62], then losing a carbonyl group, hydroxyl group, and ethyl group to generate P6 (m/z = 170.11). Following pathway III, an intramolecular SN2 substitution reaction may occur, leading to the formation of the intermediate P7 with a mass-to-charge ratio of 318.30, which has a five-membered heterocyclic ring [63]. Subsequently, under the continuous attack of ·OH, decarboxylation and defluorination occur. Furthermore, the ethylenic linkage is disrupted, similar to the formation process of P5, and the five-membered ring is oxidized by h+, ·O2 − and SO4 -·to yield an amino group forming P9 (m/z = 192.14). In pathway IV, a quinolone ring is initially opened under the continuous attack of h+, ·O2 − and SO4 -·and converted to the intermediate P10 (m/z = 324.20). The intermediate P11(m/z = 227.17) is generated after the decarboxylation of the intermediate P10 and conversion of the piperazine ring to an amino group [64]. This is followed by the hydrogenation of cyclopropyl and replacement of F with hydroxyl, resulting in product P13 (m/z = 141.11). In addition, these small molecules will eventually be mineralized by h+, ·O2 −, SO4 -·, and ·OH into CO2, H2O, and inorganic materials composed of nitrogen and fluorine.

In summary, a ternary NCQDs/Ag3PO4/Ag photocatalyst with dual-channel charge transfer property was constructed by combining NCQDs/Ag3PO4 S-scheme heterojunction and Ag3PO4/Ag Schottky junction. The synergistic effect of the ternary photocatalyst resulted in excellent CIP degradation performance. After 5 cycles in the PS activated system, the CIP removal rate was still as high as 86.73 %. The photocatalytic process fully utilized reactive radicals h+, ·O2 −, ·OH, and SO4 -·while dramatically reducing the recombination rate of photogenerated carriers. Electrons were transferred from NCQDs to Ag3PO4 driven by the IEF. Visible light illumination facilitated the transfer of photogenerated electrons through the recombination of electrons from the conduction band on Ag3PO4 and holes from the valence band on NCQDs. Additionally, Ag nanoparticles affixed to Ag3PO4 absorbed a large number of electrons and made full use of ·O2 −, contributing to the dual-channel action of S-scheme heterojunction and Schottky junction. Furthermore, the factors influencing CIP photocatalytic degradation performance as well as natural water sources were investigated, and possible degradation pathways of CIP were proposed. This work offers insights into structure-property design, mechanism explanation, and practical application potential for Ag3PO4-based photocatalysts in natural environment restoration applications.
